sentences.text: Yes.
About five weeks.
They were a little bit better.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they-- they just had better living conditions.
And they were also dressed better, the people, than we were.
Yes.
In Stuttgart?
Yes.
In a munition factory.
It wasn't bad.
Fairly well.
Oh, they-- they were much better, too.
Yes.
No.
They were like-- yes, bunks.
Yes, they were bunks.
Also about 10.
Equivalent to like they were in Birkenau.
I don't remember offhand.
Pardon me?
No.
We were just-- they just decided that there's gonna be a [GERMAN], and whoever they gonna pick, they'll go.
And I was looking for my aunts, but I didn't see them.
I have one living aunt.
She's 86 years old.
We-- we were still in-- in Birkenau when we heard that there was an attempt of-- somebody want to kill Hitler but didn't succeed, unfortunately.
The same thing as before.
We were put on a train with a German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] SS woman.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they took us on a train again.
January, February, March.
March 15, they took us on a train again.
And she was there also.
Still with the aspirins, just in case.
That same woman, yeah.
Water, they gave us.
They gave us some halfway decent food.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
No.
All woman.
No.
I would say about day and-- day and a half.
I didn't see any dead people.
We just were told to get out, and that's it.
No.
Where?
They-- we were taken to Nordhausen-Dora.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they laid us-- the three sick girls with the typhus, with the-- with the fever, and they put us on a-- on-- in a corner of a theater.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And all of a sudden, I heard men talking.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one said to the other in Czechoslovakian, I think this one, we will-- we'll be able to save.
But not the other two.
The other twos are-- are-- are too far gone.
And sure, they took us to a so-called hospital in-- in Dora there, in Nordhausen-Dora.
And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they-- he was a doctor before, you know.
He was also from Hungary.
Yeah.
And he said, what is your name?
And I told him.
And he said, where do you come from?
I told him from Bilky.
He says, I had a very nice lady.
She was my patient.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was an obstetrician.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
He said, her name was-- my mother's name was Esther Jacob.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Did you know her?
I says, yeah.
She-- she was my mother.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He says, how many of you are here?
I said, I'm the only one from that family left.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was very, very nice to me.
And then there was an incident in Dora, day after we arrived in-- in Dora, there was-- they decided to take all the Jews-- it was towards the end of the war.
And they took all the men away, and they sent them to Bergen-Belsen.
I didn't know until I was in Germany with Eric, and people would take us for-- to see the sceneries.
And once-- this was a couple years ago-- we were there in Hildesheim.
And he stopped the car on a hill.
And I looked down, and I said to him-- I says, these look like the-- the Harz Mountains.
And this man says to me, how would you know the Harz Mountains?
I says, I lived there.
What do you mean, you lived there?
I says, I lived there.
I says, I-- you know Dora, Nordhausen-Dora?
He says, yeah, I know.
I says, that's where I lived.
He says, when?
I says, when I was liberated in '45.
[LAUGHS] So you arrived in Dora in March of 1945.
Right.
Yeah.
It-- it was all right.
Because those two prisoners who found-- found out there on the floor in the theater-- on-- on the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and they were constantly knocking on the window.
We have some food for you.
We have [INAUDIBLE].
And that's-- that's how I survived.
And there was also a Czechoslovakian doctor in Dora who went up in the crematorium chimney, made sure that there is no-- there's not gonna be any people burning.
And he sent out to-- to the Allies, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, the-- where they are and-- and where they should go and everything.
And he was supposed to be hanged.
There was already-- they were talking about-- because we all had to watch when somebody was electrocuted or something.
We had to watch that.
But it turned out that when they came in, the Americans, on April 11, and right away they asked for him.
And they got together.
It is now what can we do for you.
You helped us so much.
He says, you can do only one thing for me.
Give me a bus from-- from Prague here.
I'm gonna take my-- all-- all my people back there.
And--
Oh, yeah.
John Cespiva.
Yeah.
Yes.
No.
Yes.
They were the-- the prisoners there, the Czechoslovakian, would constantly come and bring me.
And I says, don't bring it.
I can't eat all that.
No.
Czechoslovakian.
Yeah.
I was laying in the hospital.
And all of a sudden, we heard noises.
The hospital was on a hill.
And across about 400, 500 feet-- excuse me-- was the crematorium.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they came in, the prisoners.
And they said-- get yourself together, he says, because you hear the-- the noise down in the valley?
The Americans just marched in, and we are liberated.
And was a-- it was a good day for us to know.
And all I remember is going out from the hospital, down, and I came about halfway, and I just passed out.
But the other ones were still behind, and they picked me up.
And they carried me in-- in a German barrack and laid me on a bed.
And it was April, and just having on that little thin dress with the lice.
And they said, you don't have to worry now anymore.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We are liberated.
I says, yeah, where are our parents?
Right away that was the first thing.
But [SIGHS] I don't know.
Somehow God gives us strength.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So this was a couple years ago.
We were in Germany.
And another thing happened.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We met in the Jewish cemetery where Eric's father-- not Eric's father, but the ashes of him, they buried there in the cemetery, his mother.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were there visiting the grave.
And he-- he remembered some neighbors where he grew up.
And he-- I looked back in-- in the cemetery.
We were already on the outside.
And there was a man with students cleaning the [YIDDISH], the headstones.
And I looked back, and I said, Eric, gee, I says.
This must be a special person.
He doesn't look Jewish.
And this is a bunch of students are cleaning and washing the headstones.
And then he came out, introduced himself to us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were talking.
But I didn't-- he didn't know Eric, or Eric didn't know him.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And we became good friends.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We call one another.
He calls us.
We call-- call him.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And I said to him, the last time I was there, I says, I need a big favor of you.
He says, you don't have to beg for it.
Just tell me what you want.
I'll do anything for you.
You know that.
I said, how could I find out where I am, how far I am?
You know, so I would like to find out, if you could find out, is [SIGHS] about liberation.
He says, you don't remember?
Oh, I says.
I remember.
It was April 11.
He says, yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 18 SECONDS]
He says, I have a friend living in Nordhausen.
She's the only one who could help us.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I say, I want to find that lady, that-- that German guard.
I say, I do anything.
I'll bring her to America.
I says, she saved my life.
He said, well, I have somebody who would be able to help us.
And sure enough, she helped us.
My name was in that transport arriving in Dora-- everything.
And I was invited last-- it-- it-- was it two years ago, for 50th-- 50th year of liberation.
I was invited to go.
Eric can't fly, so I took my Esther with me.
And we met her there.
And I want to thank you very much, I says, but I'll thank you again a million times.
Find me that lady.
I say, I want to reciprocate for saving my life.
But as of now, I talk to her.
I write to her.
And she always says, you know, Lili, as soon as I find [? up-- ?] find out something, I'll let you know.
Yeah.
I'm--
On April 11, 1945.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the other prisoners picked you up and took you to a German barrack and laid you down on a bed because you were so weak.
Right.
Well, they just tried their best, and--
Americans.
And no sooner were-- were we liberated, I was taken again to the hospital there to-- to lay in the-- on a bed.
And a German soldier came over.
He says, you're so sick.
Where's the doctor?
I says, I don't know.
I don't know.
But I-- I couldn't speak to him in English because I didn't speak English.
Yeah.
An American soldier.
So going back to Nordhausen-Dora, like I said, we were invited to go to the anniversary, to celebrate the 50th anniversary.
And this is-- this other American soldier is standing there.
This was now, last April.
And he said, miss, do you speak English?
I said, a little bit.
I says, you know, 50 years ago today we liberated this camp.
And I didn't know where to go.
I ran to the crematorium.
I never saw anything like it.
I ran to all-- all over, to the German barracks, and all I hear is noises.
I-- so [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they took me back to the hospital.
But going back to April 11 of this-- this is '96, '95, right?
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So he said to me, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 50 years ago today we liberated this camp.
And there was-- I don't know where to run, to crematorium, to the hospital, or where.
I come to the hospital, and I see this young lady screaming, hysterical, burning up with fever.
Would you by any chance know what happened to that girl?
I looked at him.
I start crying.
I says, sir.
I am that little girl.
And in my honor, they opened up the theater for the city in Nordhausen.
What else happened on the day of liberation?
I think I was in love.
I wouldn't know, really.
About three weeks after liberation.
And he got the bus to go back to Prague and come back for his people.
And unfortunately, he-- he got cancer, and he passed away.
But I used to go to Prague a-- a-- a lot.
And God forbid if I came to Prague because of the papers coming, being able to come to America.
And God forbid if I came to Prague and he found out that-- he would call my-- the lawyer, because I would go to the lawyer to help us get the papers.
He-- he would call him.
Was she in town?
Was she in-- in the city?
And I told him, tell him the truth.
Yes or no.
He was a good friend.
So you remained in Nordhausen for another three weeks?
Yes.
And then I--
During that time?
Not much.
Yes.
I came back to the living.
Then there was another prisoner, but-- because with the Czechoslovakians, we got along.
And he came over and he says, you know, you come with me.
I'm taking you home.
Because I already talked to my wife on the telephone.
And we gonna nurse you back till you get real healthy.
And I said, but what will your wife think?
He says, don't worry what she'll think.
You'll see what kind of wife I had.
He was, you know, a-- a-- always against the government, communism.
They arrested him.
And he used to go to Auschwitz and bring me-- send me always pictures from there, you know, in-- in Auschwitz.
There's-- they are from each country like Hebrew and Hungarian, Czechoslovakian written.
You were there, so I don't have to tell you.
But it was, [?
I think, really ?] in Auschwitz, the-- I went back the first time.
Is-- it was like a bad dream.
I went back with him.
It's in Moravia.
Oh, well, we were-- we were able to get on trains without paying.
And I-- they-- they told us that whatever you see, whatever you want, you just go ahead and get it, and-- and-- and take it.
About-- about two days.
Because we came to Prague, and from Prague it wasn't that far already.
We came in very late at night.
But we-- he notified his wife that we are on our way.
And some friends were-- of his were able to take us by car to his house, to their house.
And I [? never ?] forget.
I got up in the morning and went to have coffee.
And all of a sudden, I-- I see a man.
And he was dressed like a policeman.
I started screaming and carrying on like a lunatic.
They didn't know what happened to me.
But finally, you know, they appeased me. 'Cause he says, look, this is [?
Mary's ?] brother.
He didn't come to hurt you.
And-- and I have the same thing in Miami when we arrived in Miami.
We were walking on [INAUDIBLE] Street there, and all of a sudden I see policeman.
Minute I saw policeman, I was screaming, I-- you know?
How long did you remain with this gentleman and his wife?
For about five weeks.
Yeah.
Baum.
Baum.
Baum.
From there, we went into-- in-- in the city there in Moravia.
And all of a-- and the trains were flocking to Europe with the survivors.
And all of a sudden, I see a friend on the train.
She hollers, what are you doing here?
I said, nothing.
I'm gonna go home soon.
I-- and she says, where is home?
I says, well, I'm going home.
I-- I'll never forgive myself.
I could have gone right away, because the same day I found that album, some American-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] well, he was a soldier.
But he had a very high rank.
And he said to me-- he start talking to me in English.
And I says, I'm sorry.
I don't speak English.
Well, what do you speak?
So I told him.
So he brought over some people who were able to translate the language.
And he said, you know, you have something of very great importance.
We need this.
Meant the album.
I-- he says, we would like very much if you would give it to us.
We gonna have trials here, and we'll need that proof.
I says, I'm not gonna give it.
If I can't go there and hold on to the album, not giving it away.
I found it in a vacant German barrack on the day of liberation.
Was in a nightstand next to where the-- my fellow prisoners left me.
And then when they came to pick me up, I says, look, I found an Auschwitz album, which was six months before-- later and 500 miles away.
Pictures of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of the people that I grew up with, that we were friends or we were relatives.
He says, you wouldn't be able to take it because they'll arrest you, maybe, somewheres on the border, and they'll take it away from you.
As it turn out, they did take it away from-- from me, the Russians.
But they gave it back 'cause I carried on like a lunatic.
Yes.
There were like 200 pictures pasted in.
Right away.
Yes.
No.
I was very ignorant about it.
I kept it with me.
When my daughter was-- our daughter was born, Esther, that album was under her mattress in the carriage.
